questionText: A technician measures a 20-cell battery string where each cell should float at 2.25V/cell. What is the expected total string voltage at proper float charge?
explanation: A 20-cell string at 2.25V/cell float voltage equals 20 × 2.25 = 45.0V. This is the expected total string voltage during normal float operation. At 2.0V/cell (nominal), the same string would measure only 40V, which indicates an undercharged or discharged condition rather than proper float.

questionText: A 12-cell battery string is measured at 25.2V during float charge. What does this reading indicate about cell condition?

choices.text: The string is properly charged at 2.10V/cell, which is within normal range
The string is overcharged; expected float is 27V
The string is undercharged; expected float voltage for 12 cells at 2.25V/cell is 27V
The reading is normal because nominal cell voltage is 2.0V/cell

explanation: A 12-cell VRLA string at proper float should measure 12 × 2.25V = 27.0V. A reading of 25.2V equates to only 2.10V/cell, indicating the string is undercharged or the charger float voltage is set too low. This condition can lead to sulfation over time and reduced battery capacity.

questionText: During individual cell voltage measurements on a 20-cell string, 19 cells read between 2.24V and 2.26V, but one cell reads 1.98V. What is the most likely diagnosis and recommended action?

choices.text: The cell is slightly low; monitor it at the next PMI
The cell has a shorted or failed plate group; the entire string should be replaced
The charger output is too low; increase the float voltage setpoint
The low cell is normal because string voltage is still within tolerance

explanation: A single cell reading 1.98V while all others measure 2.24–2.26V indicates that cell has a failed or shorted plate group and is not accepting or holding charge. Per best practice, when a weak cell is identified in a string, the entire string must be replaced simultaneously. Replacing only the weak cell and mixing it with older cells causes uneven discharge and premature failure of the new cell.

questionText: A UPS battery string is rated for a 60-minute capacity. During a discharge test, the string reaches the 1.75V/cell endpoint at 44 minutes. What action is required?

choices.text: No action; the battery passed because it lasted more than 30 minutes
Replace the battery string; it reached endpoint in less than 80% of rated time (48 minutes)
Perform an equalize charge and retest before deciding on replacement
Adjust the endpoint voltage to 1.67V/cell to extend the test duration

explanation: The replacement threshold is 80% of rated capacity time. For a 60-minute rated string, 80% equals 48 minutes. The battery reached the 1.75V/cell endpoint at 44 minutes, which is less than 48 minutes, so the battery fails the capacity test and must be replaced. Adjusting the endpoint voltage is not acceptable; 1.75V/cell is the industry-standard discharge endpoint.

questionText: A battery string rated for a 30-minute runtime reaches the 1.75V/cell endpoint in 25 minutes. What is the percentage of rated capacity delivered, and what is the correct determination?

choices.text: 83.3%; the battery passes because it exceeded 80% of rated time
75.0%; the battery fails because it did not reach 80% of rated time
83.3%; the battery fails because it did not reach 85% of rated time
75.0%; the battery passes because the margin is within acceptable tolerance

explanation: The battery delivered 25 out of 30 minutes, which is 25/30 × 100 = 83.3% of rated capacity time. The IEEE replacement threshold is 80% of rated capacity. Since 83.3% exceeds 80%, the battery passes this capacity test. The battery should continue to be monitored, but replacement is not yet mandatory based on this result.

questionText: What is the standard discharge endpoint voltage per cell used during a UPS battery capacity test?

choices.text: 2.00V/cell
1.80V/cell
1.75V/cell
1.67V/cell

explanation: The industry-standard and IEEE-recommended discharge endpoint for UPS lead-acid batteries during a capacity test is 1.75V/cell. Discharging below this level risks permanent damage to the battery plates through deep sulfation. The test is terminated when any cell or the string average reaches this threshold, and the elapsed time is compared to the rated runtime.

questionText: A conductance test on a VRLA cell returns a very low conductance reading compared to the manufacturer's baseline. What does this indicate?

choices.text: The cell is in excellent condition with very low internal resistance
The cell has high impedance and is degraded; it should be flagged for replacement
The conductance test is not applicable to VRLA cells
The cell requires an equalize charge to restore conductance

explanation: Conductance and impedance are inversely related: low conductance equals high impedance. A degraded battery cell develops increased internal resistance due to plate corrosion, sulfation, or electrolyte loss, which reduces its conductance value. When a cell measures significantly below the manufacturer's baseline conductance (typically more than 25% below), it is considered degraded and should be flagged for replacement without requiring a full discharge test.

questionText: What is the primary advantage of an AC conductance test over a full discharge capacity test for evaluating battery health?

choices.text: Conductance tests are more accurate than capacity tests
Conductance tests measure cell health without fully discharging the battery, keeping the UPS available
Conductance tests can identify the exact remaining capacity in amp-hours
Conductance tests can be performed while the battery is disconnected from the UPS

explanation: The AC conductance test applies a small AC signal to each cell and measures the response to calculate conductance without discharging the battery. This means the UPS battery string remains available for use throughout the test, unlike a full discharge capacity test which takes the battery offline and requires a load bank. The tradeoff is that conductance testing is a trend indicator, not a definitive capacity measurement.

questionText: During a string conductance survey, one cell measures 180 Siemens while the manufacturer's baseline for a new cell of this type is 300 Siemens. What percentage of baseline is this cell at, and what action is indicated?

choices.text: 60% of baseline; the cell is degraded and should be flagged for replacement
60% of baseline; the cell is within acceptable range because it is above 50%
167% of baseline; the cell has higher than expected impedance
40% below baseline; no action needed until next annual PMI

explanation: The cell measures 180/300 = 60% of the manufacturer's baseline conductance value. A cell at 60% of its original conductance has lost 40% of its conductance, indicating significant internal degradation. Industry practice flags cells at 75–80% of baseline or below as suspect, and cells at 60% of baseline are clearly degraded and should be flagged for replacement to prevent unexpected failure during a power outage.

questionText: A VRLA battery room is operating at 30°C ambient instead of the standard 25°C. Using the temperature coefficient of −0.003V/°C/cell, what should the adjusted float voltage per cell be if the nominal setpoint is 2.27V/cell at 25°C?

choices.text: 2.285V/cell
2.255V/cell
2.240V/cell
2.300V/cell

explanation: The temperature coefficient is −0.003V/°C/cell. The temperature increase is 30°C − 25°C = 5°C. The voltage correction is 5 × (−0.003) = −0.015V/cell. Adjusted float voltage = 2.27 − 0.015 = 2.255V/cell. At elevated temperatures, VRLA batteries absorb more current at a given voltage, so reducing the float voltage prevents overcharging, excessive gassing, electrolyte loss, and thermal runaway.

questionText: Which sequence of events correctly describes VRLA thermal runaway?

choices.text: High temperature → reduced charge acceptance → less current → cooling
Elevated temperature → increased charge acceptance → more current → more heat → runaway
Overcharge → reduced temperature → increased gassing → electrolyte loss
Low temperature → high impedance → excess voltage → thermal runaway

explanation: VRLA thermal runaway is a self-reinforcing positive feedback loop: as battery temperature rises, the internal chemistry causes the battery to accept more charging current at the same float voltage. This increased current generates additional heat, which further raises temperature, which further increases charge acceptance. Without temperature compensation or a thermal protection system, this cycle escalates until the battery vents, swells, or ignites.

questionText: A UPS battery room temperature sensor shows 35°C. The charger float voltage is set to 2.27V/cell with no temperature compensation. Using a coefficient of −0.003V/°C/cell, how many millivolts per cell should the float be reduced?

choices.text: 10 mV/cell
15 mV/cell
30 mV/cell
3 mV/cell

explanation: The temperature deviation from the 25°C standard is 35°C − 25°C = 10°C. Applying the coefficient: 10°C × 0.003V/°C/cell = 0.030V/cell = 30 mV/cell reduction. The corrected float voltage should be 2.27V − 0.030V = 2.240V/cell. Without this reduction, the elevated temperature causes the battery to accept excess current, increasing the risk of thermal runaway.

questionText: What is the correct float voltage range for VRLA batteries?
explanation: VRLA (valve-regulated lead-acid) batteries are maintained at a float voltage of 2.25–2.27V/cell. This range maintains full charge while minimizing water loss through electrolysis and gassing. The higher range of 2.40–2.45V/cell is the equalize voltage used only for flooded lead-acid cells to reverse sulfation, and it must never be applied to VRLA batteries as it causes overcharge, venting, and thermal runaway.

questionText: A technician attempts to perform an equalize charge on a VRLA battery string to reverse sulfation. Why is this procedure incorrect and dangerous?

choices.text: Equalize charging is too slow for VRLA chemistry and will not reverse sulfation
VRLA batteries are sealed; equalize voltage (2.40–2.45V/cell) causes overcharge, venting, electrolyte loss, and thermal runaway risk
Equalize charging requires special connectors not compatible with VRLA terminals
Equalize charging at 2.40V/cell is acceptable for VRLA if done for less than one hour

explanation: VRLA batteries use a recombinant design where oxygen generated at the positive plate recombines with hydrogen at the negative plate, keeping them sealed. Applying equalize voltage (2.40–2.45V/cell) forces excessive current through the cells, generating gas faster than the recombination reaction can handle. This overpressurizes the cells, opens the safety vents, releases hydrogen gas, causes electrolyte loss, and creates conditions for thermal runaway. Equalize charging is only safe for flooded cells with removable vent caps.

questionText: For flooded lead-acid cells showing sulfation, what is the correct equalize voltage range, and which safety precaution is mandatory during this procedure?

choices.text: 2.25–2.27V/cell; ensure temperature compensation is active
2.40–2.45V/cell; ensure battery room ventilation is adequate to exhaust hydrogen gas
2.40–2.45V/cell; seal the battery room to prevent oxygen from entering
2.30–2.35V/cell; monitor cell temperatures every 15 minutes

explanation: Equalize charging for flooded lead-acid cells is performed at 2.40–2.45V/cell to reverse sulfation by providing a controlled overcharge. During this process, electrolysis splits water into hydrogen and oxygen gas, which is vented through the removable cell caps. Hydrogen is highly flammable at concentrations above 4% in air, so adequate ventilation is mandatory to keep hydrogen concentrations well below the lower explosive limit. Sealed rooms during equalize charging create an explosion hazard.

questionText: A UPS has a battery string where two modules have failed. A technician proposes replacing only the two failed modules and leaving the remaining eight original modules in service. What is the problem with this approach?

choices.text: There is no problem; replacing only failed modules is the most cost-effective approach
Mixing new and old battery modules causes uneven discharge, premature failure of the new modules, and unreliable runtime estimates
The two new modules will charge faster and carry more load, improving overall performance
Partial replacement is acceptable if the new modules are from the same manufacturer

explanation: Battery modules in a series string must be replaced as a complete set. New modules have significantly higher capacity and different internal resistance than aged modules. During discharge, the older, weaker modules reach their endpoint voltage first while the new modules still have capacity remaining. This causes the weak modules to be driven into reversal (negative voltage), permanently damaging them. Additionally, during charge, the new modules may be overcharged while the old ones are still being charged, accelerating degradation of the entire string.

questionText: When replacing a UPS battery string, what is the correct practice regarding battery age and sourcing?

choices.text: New batteries can be mixed with batteries up to two years old if they are the same model number
All cells or modules in a string must be replaced simultaneously with new batteries of the same type
It is acceptable to use refurbished batteries in the same string as new batteries to reduce cost
Batteries from different manufacturers can be mixed if the voltage ratings match

explanation: Best practice and most UPS manufacturer specifications require that all cells or modules in a string be replaced at the same time with new batteries of the same type and from the same manufacturing lot when possible. This ensures uniform internal resistance, capacity, and age across the entire string. Mixing batteries of different ages or sources causes the weakest battery to limit the entire string's performance and leads to accelerated failure of both old and new components.

questionText: What is the primary function of a Battery Management System (BMS) in a lithium-ion UPS battery?

choices.text: To convert DC battery voltage to AC for the UPS inverter
To monitor and control cell voltage, temperature, and current to prevent overcharge, overdischarge, and thermal runaway
To equalize the charge between parallel battery strings
To provide a communication interface between the battery and the building management system

explanation: The Battery Management System (BMS) is a critical safety and control component in lithium-ion battery systems. It continuously monitors individual cell voltages, temperatures, and charge/discharge current. If any cell approaches overcharge voltage, the BMS reduces or cuts off charging current. If any cell approaches over-discharge cutoff, the BMS disconnects the load. The BMS also detects abnormal temperature rise as an early indicator of thermal runaway and can trigger an emergency shutdown, making it essential for safe Li-ion operation.

questionText: Compared to VRLA lead-acid batteries, which characteristic correctly describes lithium-ion batteries used in UPS systems?

choices.text: Lower energy density, shorter service life, slower recharge time
Higher energy density, longer service life, faster recharge time
Higher energy density, shorter service life, same recharge time
Lower energy density, longer service life, faster recharge time

explanation: Lithium-ion batteries offer significant advantages over VRLA in UPS applications: higher energy density means the same energy capacity can be stored in a smaller, lighter package; service life is typically 10–15 years versus 4–6 years for VRLA; and recharge time is much faster, often reaching 80% charge in under an hour versus 8–12 hours for lead-acid. These advantages come with higher upfront cost and the requirement for a BMS, but the total cost of ownership is often lower over the installation lifetime.

questionText: A Li-ion UPS battery cabinet goes offline with a BMS fault alarm. The BMS log shows a single cell at 4.25V while all others are at 3.65V. What is the most likely cause, and what is the correct immediate action?

choices.text: Cell imbalance from normal use; perform a cell balancing cycle and return to service
Overcharge of one cell likely due to a BMS balancing circuit failure; isolate the battery cabinet and do not recharge until inspected by qualified personnel
The BMS voltage sensor is faulty; replace the sensor and clear the fault
Normal variation; Li-ion cells operate safely up to 4.5V

explanation: A single Li-ion cell at 4.25V while others are at 3.65V indicates that cell has been overcharged, likely due to a failure in the BMS active balancing circuit that normally limits individual cell voltage to around 4.2V (for NMC chemistry). An overcharged Li-ion cell is at elevated risk of thermal runaway, separator breakdown, and fire. The correct immediate action is to isolate the battery cabinet from both the charger and the load, and not attempt to recharge until a qualified technician has inspected and repaired the BMS.

questionText: What is the memory effect in nickel-cadmium (Ni-Cd) batteries, and how does it affect UPS battery performance?

choices.text: The battery retains a charge memory from previous discharge cycles, allowing it to deliver more capacity over time
If repeatedly recharged before fully discharging, the battery develops a false discharge endpoint, reducing its effective capacity
The battery stores data about previous charge cycles in an internal memory chip
The memory effect causes Ni-Cd batteries to overcharge if not cycled regularly

explanation: The memory effect in Ni-Cd batteries occurs when the battery is repeatedly recharged without being fully discharged first. The battery chemistry forms stable crystalline structures at the point of repeated early recharge, causing the battery to behave as if it has a lower capacity than its rated specification. In UPS applications, this manifests as premature low-battery alarms and shorter-than-rated runtime. The remedy is a full discharge cycle followed by a full recharge, which can partially restore lost capacity.

questionText: Which of the following is a key advantage of Ni-Cd batteries compared to VRLA lead-acid in extreme environment UPS applications?

choices.text: Ni-Cd batteries have higher energy density, making them lighter for the same capacity
Ni-Cd batteries operate reliably across extreme temperatures and have a very high cycle life
Ni-Cd batteries do not require a battery management system unlike lithium-ion
Ni-Cd batteries are more environmentally friendly than lead-acid

explanation: Ni-Cd batteries excel in extreme temperature environments, maintaining capacity in conditions where VRLA batteries would fail, such as very high or very low ambient temperatures. They also have an exceptionally high cycle life, often exceeding 1,500 cycles compared to 200–500 for VRLA. These properties make them preferred in outdoor telecom cabinets, industrial environments, and applications where temperature control is not available. The significant disadvantage is that cadmium is highly toxic and the batteries require special disposal procedures under hazardous waste regulations.

questionText: During disposal of failed Ni-Cd batteries from a UPS system, a junior technician proposes throwing them in the regular trash. What is the correct response?

choices.text: This is acceptable if the batteries are discharged below 1.0V/cell first
Ni-Cd batteries contain toxic cadmium and must be disposed of as hazardous waste through a certified recycler or return program, never in regular trash
Ni-Cd batteries can go in electronic recycling bins at any retail store
Only the electrolyte needs special disposal; the battery case can be discarded normally

explanation: Cadmium is a heavy metal classified as a hazardous material under EPA regulations and RCRA. Even small amounts of cadmium leaching from landfills can contaminate groundwater. Ni-Cd batteries must be disposed of through certified hazardous waste handlers or manufacturer take-back programs. Many jurisdictions also have regulations prohibiting landfill disposal of Ni-Cd batteries. FSEs must ensure proper disposal documentation is provided to the customer and that batteries are transported in appropriate containers with proper hazardous material labeling.

questionText: What test voltage should be used when performing insulation resistance testing with a megohmmeter on standard 480V low-voltage UPS wiring?

choices.text: 250V DC
500V DC
1000V DC
2500V DC

explanation: For low-voltage equipment and wiring (typically rated 600V and below, including standard 480V UPS installations), the correct megohmmeter test voltage is 500V DC. Using 1000V DC is appropriate for medium-voltage equipment (above 600V). Applying too high a test voltage to low-voltage insulation can overstress and damage the insulation being tested, producing false failures or causing actual insulation breakdown.

questionText: A megohmmeter test on a UPS transformer winding to ground returns a reading of 0.8MΩ. What does this indicate?

choices.text: Excellent insulation; readings below 1MΩ indicate very low resistance to current flow
Dangerous insulation condition; readings below 1MΩ indicate severely degraded insulation with risk of ground fault
The reading is within acceptable range; good insulation is typically 0.5–1.0MΩ
The transformer insulation is new; new insulation always reads below 1MΩ

explanation: Good electrical insulation should measure well above 100MΩ on a megohmmeter. A reading of 0.8MΩ is in the danger zone (below 1MΩ), indicating severely degraded insulation that has likely absorbed moisture, carbonized from overheating, or been physically damaged. At this level, the insulation provides very little resistance to leakage current and poses a serious risk of ground fault, arc flash, and electric shock. The equipment must be taken out of service and the insulation repaired or the component replaced before energizing.

questionText: Before performing a megohmmeter test on a UPS output transformer, which preparatory step is absolutely required?

choices.text: Set the UPS to bypass mode so the transformer remains energized during testing
Disconnect the equipment under test from all power sources and discharge any capacitance before applying the megohmmeter
Connect the megohmmeter neutral lead to the transformer core before applying test voltage
Apply the test voltage for at least 10 minutes to get an accurate reading

explanation: Performing insulation resistance testing with a megohmmeter on energized equipment is extremely dangerous and will damage the test instrument. The equipment under test must be completely de-energized, isolated under LOTO, and all capacitance in the circuit (from capacitor banks or long cable runs) must be fully discharged before connecting the megohmmeter. Residual capacitive charge can cause the megohmmeter to give erroneous readings, and connecting it to a charged capacitor can damage the instrument or cause injury. After testing, the tested circuit must be discharged again through the megohmmeter ground lead before disconnecting.

questionText: What minimum insulation resistance reading is generally considered acceptable for well-maintained electrical insulation during a megohmmeter test?

choices.text: Greater than 1MΩ
Greater than 10MΩ
Greater than 100MΩ
Greater than 500MΩ

explanation: Industry standards and IEEE 43 recommend that good insulation should measure above 100MΩ. Readings between 1MΩ and 100MΩ are considered questionable and warrant investigation, trend monitoring, or further testing. Readings below 1MΩ are in the danger zone requiring immediate action. New or recently serviced equipment often reads in the gigaohm (1000+ MΩ) range. Trending the insulation resistance over time is more informative than a single reading, as a declining trend toward 100MΩ indicates progressive degradation.

questionText: Why does a DLRO (Digital Low Resistance Ohmmeter) use a 4-wire Kelvin connection instead of a standard 2-wire connection?

choices.text: To allow the test to be performed safely on energized equipment
To eliminate the resistance of the test leads from the measurement so only the resistance of the device under test is measured
To apply higher test current for more accurate readings on large conductors
To simultaneously measure resistance and capacitance of the connection

explanation: The Kelvin 4-wire method uses separate current-injection and voltage-sensing lead pairs. The DLRO injects a known DC current (e.g., 10A) through the outer current leads while measuring the voltage drop with the inner sensing leads. Because the sensing leads carry virtually no current, their resistance does not contribute to the voltage measurement. This allows the instrument to measure resistances in the milliohm range accurately, which would otherwise be masked by the resistance of the test leads themselves (typically a few hundred milliohms in a standard 2-wire setup).

questionText: A DLRO measurement on a main circuit breaker contact returns 850 microohms (µΩ). The manufacturer's specification calls for less than 200µΩ. What does this indicate?

choices.text: The breaker contacts are in good condition; 850µΩ is within normal variation
The breaker contacts are significantly degraded; high resistance indicates pitting, oxidation, or loose connections that will generate heat under load
The DLRO reading is invalid; circuit breakers cannot be tested with a DLRO
The reading indicates the breaker is oversized for the application

explanation: A contact resistance of 850µΩ is more than four times the manufacturer's maximum specification of 200µΩ. Elevated contact resistance in a circuit breaker results from pitting, oxidation, carbon deposits, or a loose mechanism. Under full load current, this excess resistance generates heat proportional to I²R, which accelerates contact degradation, can trip thermal-magnetic breakers at below-rated currents, and in severe cases causes overheating at the connection point. The breaker should be scheduled for replacement.

questionText: During a DLRO test of a busbar joint, the technician applies 10A of DC current and the instrument measures 8.5mV across the joint. What is the resistance of the joint in milliohms?

choices.text: 8.5mΩ
85mΩ

explanation: Using Ohm's Law: R = V / I = 8.5mV / 10A = 0.85mΩ (milliohms). The DLRO applies a known current and measures the resulting voltage drop, then calculates resistance. Good busbar joint connections typically measure below 1mΩ. A reading of 0.85mΩ is likely acceptable, but should be compared to adjacent joints and manufacturer specifications. If this reading were significantly higher than similar joints on the same busbar, it would indicate a problem with that connection.

questionText: Which of the following connection points is the most appropriate application for a DLRO measurement during a UPS PMI?

choices.text: Measuring the insulation resistance of the UPS output transformer
Measuring the resistance of battery inter-cell connectors and cable terminations
Measuring the output voltage accuracy of the UPS inverter
Measuring the capacitance of DC bus filter capacitors

explanation: The DLRO is specifically designed to measure very low resistance connections in the milliohm range. Battery inter-cell connectors and cable terminations are prime targets because loose or corroded connections develop elevated resistance that causes voltage drop, localized heating, and reduced available battery voltage during discharge. DLRO measurements provide an objective baseline and allow trending over successive PMIs to identify connections that are trending toward failure before they cause problems.

questionText: When measuring DC bus ripple voltage with an oscilloscope, why must AC coupling mode be used instead of DC coupling?

choices.text: AC coupling allows the oscilloscope to measure higher voltages safely
AC coupling blocks the large DC offset so the small AC ripple component can be displayed at an appropriate scale for accurate measurement
DC coupling would damage the oscilloscope when connected to a DC bus
AC coupling is required to trigger the oscilloscope on the ripple frequency

explanation: A UPS DC bus may operate at several hundred volts DC. The ripple voltage is a small AC component superimposed on this large DC level, typically measuring only a few volts peak-to-peak. In DC coupling mode, the vertical scale must accommodate the full DC voltage, making the tiny ripple signal invisible at any usable resolution. AC coupling inserts a capacitor in series with the input, blocking the DC component so only the AC ripple appears on screen, allowing the technician to zoom in with appropriate vertical sensitivity to accurately measure peak-to-peak ripple amplitude.

questionText: A UPS DC bus operates at 540V DC. The oscilloscope in AC coupling mode shows a peak-to-peak ripple of 12V. What is the ripple percentage, and what does this indicate?

choices.text: 2.2%; within acceptable limits
2.2%; exceeds the 2% threshold, indicating potential capacitor degradation
4.5%; severely exceeds acceptable limits, immediate capacitor replacement required
12%; the DC bus voltage regulation has failed

explanation: DC bus ripple percentage is calculated as: (peak-to-peak ripple / DC bus voltage) × 100 = (12V / 540V) × 100 = 2.22%. This exceeds the commonly accepted threshold of 2% of DC bus voltage. Elevated ripple is caused by degraded DC bus electrolytic capacitors that have lost capacitance due to age, thermal stress, or voltage stress. These capacitors should be evaluated for replacement, as high ripple can stress the inverter switching devices, increase conducted EMI on the output, and shorten the service life of other components.

questionText: High DC bus ripple voltage in a UPS is most commonly caused by which component failure?

choices.text: Failed input rectifier diodes
Degraded or failed DC bus electrolytic filter capacitors
Open-circuit battery fuse
Failed output transformer

explanation: DC bus filter capacitors are the primary component that smooths the rectified DC bus voltage by storing and releasing charge to fill in the voltage valleys between rectifier pulses. As electrolytic capacitors age, they lose capacitance (ESR increases), reducing their ability to filter ripple. When capacitors degrade, the peak-to-peak ripple on the DC bus increases. High ripple stresses inverter switching devices (IGBTs or MOSFETs) with higher peak currents, increases audible noise, and introduces harmonic content on the UPS output voltage waveform.

questionText: When measuring output currents of a 3-phase UPS with a clamp meter, Phase A reads 48A, Phase B reads 47A, and Phase C reads 38A. What is the approximate phase current imbalance, and what action is indicated?

choices.text: Approximately 2% imbalance; within acceptable range, no action needed
Approximately 21% imbalance on Phase C relative to the average; investigate the load distribution and check for winding fault
Approximately 10A difference; this is normal for varying loads
Approximately 5% imbalance; monitor at next PMI

explanation: Average current = (48 + 47 + 38) / 3 = 44.3A. Phase C deviation = (44.3 − 38) / 44.3 × 100 = 14.2%. The maximum deviation from average exceeds 5%, which is the threshold that warrants investigation. Options include checking for unbalanced single-phase loads connected across individual phases, verifying Phase C output wiring and terminal tightness, and investigating the possibility of a winding fault in the output transformer or filter inductor on Phase C. Significant imbalance causes neutral current, increased heating in distribution wiring, and reduced equipment efficiency.

questionText: What phase current imbalance threshold should prompt a field service technician to investigate for unbalanced loads or winding faults in a 3-phase UPS?

choices.text: Greater than 1%
Greater than 5%
Greater than 15%
Greater than 25%

explanation: A phase current imbalance exceeding 5% between any phase and the average of all three phases warrants investigation. NEMA and IEEE guidelines generally use a 5% threshold as the point at which imbalance begins to cause measurable increases in motor heating (approximately 6–10°C per percent of imbalance above 1%). In UPS systems, current imbalance can indicate unbalanced single-phase loads on the distribution, winding asymmetry in transformers, or individual phase wiring or connection problems. Identifying and correcting imbalance protects downstream equipment and improves UPS efficiency.

questionText: A UPS output neutral current measurement reads 32A on a 3-phase 4-wire 120/208V system. The three phase currents are each approximately 45A. What is the most likely cause of this neutral current?

choices.text: A ground fault on the UPS output; neutral current should always be zero
Non-linear (switching) loads generating triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) that add in the neutral conductor rather than canceling
The neutral wire is undersized and creating back-EMF in the circuit
Phase imbalance; neutral current is proportional to the difference between phase currents

explanation: In a balanced 3-phase system with linear loads, fundamental (60Hz) currents cancel in the neutral. However, non-linear loads such as switch-mode power supplies in IT equipment generate harmonic currents, particularly triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th order harmonics). Unlike fundamental and other harmonics, triplen harmonics are in phase with each other across all three phases and add arithmetically in the neutral conductor instead of canceling. In heavily loaded data center environments, neutral current can approach or exceed the phase current. This requires that neutral conductors be sized at 200% capacity in electronic load applications.

questionText: What is the correct sequence of steps for performing a UPS preventive maintenance inspection (PMI)?

choices.text: Functional tests → electrical measurements → visual inspection → mechanical checks → restore to service
Visual inspection → electrical measurements → mechanical checks → functional tests → restore to service
Electrical measurements → visual inspection → functional tests → mechanical checks → restore to service
Mechanical checks → visual inspection → electrical measurements → functional tests → restore to service

explanation: The correct PMI sequence begins with visual inspection to identify obvious hazards (leaks, burn marks, loose wires) before touching anything, then proceeds to electrical measurements (voltages, currents, insulation resistance) with appropriate safety measures, followed by mechanical checks (torque, fan operation, filter condition), then functional tests (battery test, transfer test) to verify correct operation, and finally restoring the system to its normal operating configuration. This sequence prevents safety hazards and ensures comprehensive coverage of all maintenance activities.

questionText: Which pre-work step must be completed before beginning any electrical measurements or mechanical work inside a UPS cabinet?

choices.text: Review the previous PMI report to compare baseline measurements
Apply LOTO (Lockout/Tagout) procedures and discharge all capacitors before opening the cabinet
Contact the customer's IT department to schedule a maintenance window
Perform a visual inspection from outside the cabinet

explanation: Before any internal access for electrical measurements or mechanical work, LOTO (Lockout/Tagout) must be applied to all energy sources per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147. UPS systems have multiple energy sources: utility input, bypass input, battery, and stored charge in large DC bus capacitors. Even after disconnecting all external power, DC bus capacitors can retain lethal voltage for minutes. The LOTO procedure must include discharging capacitors and verifying zero energy with a voltage meter before touching internal components. Reviewing the previous PMI report is a pre-work planning step but is not a safety prerequisite for entering the cabinet.

questionText: During a UPS PMI pre-work review, a technician notes the previous PMI report shows DC bus capacitors were replaced 18 months ago and battery conductance values were trending downward across 3 years of measurements. What actions should the technician prioritize based on this review?

choices.text: Focus on the capacitors since they were recently replaced and may still be under warranty
Prioritize a new conductance survey to continue the battery trend data and determine if replacement is now warranted based on the degradation trajectory
Skip the battery test since conductance testing was clearly done at previous PMIs
Replace the battery string immediately without testing since three years of declining values indicates failure

explanation: Reviewing previous PMI reports allows the technician to identify trends, not just single-point measurements. A three-year downward trend in battery conductance values is a significant finding that requires updated conductance measurements at this PMI to continue the trend line and determine the current rate of degradation. If the current values have dropped to or below the manufacturer's replacement threshold (typically 75–80% of baseline), replacement can be justified. If the trend is accelerating, earlier replacement may be recommended even if values have not yet crossed the threshold.

questionText: Why should spare parts be gathered and staged before beginning a UPS PMI, rather than ordering them after finding a problem?

choices.text: To comply with OSHA regulations requiring spare parts on-site before maintenance begins
To minimize system downtime by enabling immediate replacement of commonly failed components discovered during the PMI
To reduce the cost of the PMI by combining shipping charges for multiple parts
Spare parts are not typically brought to a PMI; they are ordered as needed

explanation: Gathering spare parts before a PMI allows commonly replaced items (fan filters, fuses, capacitors, battery inter-cell connectors) to be replaced immediately if found defective during the inspection. Without pre-staged spares, a technician who discovers a failed cooling fan must leave the system in a degraded state while waiting for parts, which extends the period of risk. Pre-staging also reduces truck rolls and customer scheduling disruptions. Experienced FSEs review the previous PMI report and known failure patterns for that UPS model to determine appropriate spare parts to bring.

questionText: A UPS is connected to three energy sources: utility AC input, bypass AC input, and a battery string. What is the minimum number of lockout points required for a proper LOTO procedure?

choices.text: One lockout point on the main utility input breaker
Two lockout points: utility input and battery disconnect
Three lockout points: utility AC input, bypass AC input, and battery disconnect
Four lockout points: utility input, bypass input, battery disconnect, and output disconnect

explanation: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 requires lockout of ALL energy sources before work begins. A typical UPS has a minimum of three separate energy inputs that can energize internal components: the main utility AC input, the static bypass AC input (which can back-feed through the static switch), and the battery string (which provides DC energy independent of AC sources). All three must have locks applied to their respective disconnect devices. Depending on the UPS architecture, some installations have additional energy sources such as distributed bypass panels that require additional lockout points.

questionText: After applying LOTO to all three energy sources of a UPS, what additional step must be performed before a technician can safely work inside the UPS cabinet?

choices.text: Wait 30 minutes for stored energy to naturally dissipate
Verify zero energy by measuring DC bus voltage and AC input terminals with an appropriate CAT-rated voltage meter
Disconnect the battery inter-cell connectors to prevent accidental reconnection
Apply a second set of locks to the same lockout points as a redundant precaution

explanation: Applying LOTO locks isolates the energy sources, but stored energy in DC bus capacitors can persist for several minutes after disconnection. OSHA's energy control procedure requires verification of zero energy state before exposure to potentially hazardous energy. The technician must use a properly rated voltage meter (CAT III or CAT IV rated for the voltage level) to verify that the DC bus voltage has discharged to zero and that all AC input terminals are de-energized. This verification step is not optional — LOTO alone does not confirm zero energy because switches can fail in closed positions or capacitors may not have fully discharged.

questionText: A 24-cell VRLA battery string is measured at 55.2V during float charge. Calculate the average voltage per cell and determine if this reading indicates a normal, low, or high float condition.

choices.text: 2.30V/cell; slightly high but within acceptable VRLA float range
2.30V/cell; above the VRLA float range of 2.25–2.27V/cell, indicating possible overcharge
2.30V/cell; this is the equalize voltage and indicates the charger is in equalize mode
2.20V/cell; below the float range, indicating undercharge

explanation: Average cell voltage = 55.2V / 24 cells = 2.30V/cell. The normal VRLA float voltage range is 2.25–2.27V/cell. At 2.30V/cell, the charger is set above the recommended float range, which will cause excess current flow into the fully charged cells, increasing heat generation, accelerating water loss, and elevating thermal runaway risk over time. The charger float voltage setpoint should be reduced to bring the string into the 2.25–2.27V/cell range. Note that 2.30V/cell is below the equalize voltage of 2.40–2.45V/cell, so this is not an equalize condition.

questionText: A battery conductance survey is performed on a 20-cell string. Cells 1–19 all measure within 10% of the manufacturer's 300S baseline. Cell 20 measures 195S. Which statement best describes the corrective action?

choices.text: Replace only Cell 20 since the remaining 19 cells are healthy
Replace the entire string simultaneously; Cell 20 at 65% of baseline is degraded and mixing new and old cells causes premature failure of the new cell
Perform an equalize charge to restore Cell 20's conductance before making a replacement decision
Monitor Cell 20 at the next quarterly PMI before recommending replacement

explanation: Cell 20 measures 195/300 = 65% of baseline, which is below the typical 75–80% replacement threshold and indicates significant degradation. However, even if the policy were to replace only the defective cell, mixing a new cell with 19 cells that are already aged causes the new cell to be overworked during discharge (since the older cells reach end voltage first) and potentially overcharged during recharge. Battery manufacturers and IEEE 1188 recommend replacing all cells in a string simultaneously to maintain uniform performance and avoid cascading failures.

questionText: A battery string is rated for 100Ah at the 8-hour rate (C8). A load test is being conducted at the C1 rate (100A). Approximately how long should the battery sustain this discharge before reaching the 1.75V/cell endpoint, assuming an ideal battery?

choices.text: 8 hours, because the battery is rated 100Ah
Approximately 1 hour at C1 rate, but expect less due to Peukert effect at higher discharge rates
Exactly 1 hour because 100Ah ÷ 100A = 1 hour
Less than 1 hour due to Peukert effect; high discharge rates reduce effective capacity significantly

explanation: The Peukert effect describes how battery effective capacity decreases at higher discharge rates. A battery rated 100Ah at the C8 rate (12.5A for 8 hours) will deliver significantly less than 100Ah when discharged at the C1 rate (100A). The actual runtime at 100A will be less than 1 hour because the higher rate causes greater internal voltage losses and accelerates depletion of the active material. Lead-acid batteries typically deliver 50–75% of their C8 rated capacity at the C1 rate. This is why UPS battery runtime specifications specify both the load and the discharge rate.

questionText: A VRLA battery cabinet shows swollen battery cases and a strong sulfurous smell. What condition does this indicate, and what is the immediate action?

choices.text: Normal outgassing during equalize charge; reduce equalize voltage and continue
Advanced thermal runaway or severe overcharge; immediately reduce or remove charging current and assess the batteries for replacement
Battery sulfation that can be reversed by an equalize charge cycle
Electrolyte stratification; tilt the batteries to remix the electrolyte

explanation: Swollen VRLA battery cases indicate internal gas pressure has built up beyond the design capacity of the recombination system, causing the case to deform. This results from excessive charging current, which is a hallmark of thermal runaway or severe overcharge. The strong sulfurous smell comes from hydrogen sulfide produced when electrolyte decomposition becomes severe. Immediate action is to reduce or completely remove the charging current to stop the heat-generating overcharge, ensure adequate ventilation (hydrogen gas risk), and evacuate personnel if the smell is strong. The batteries should be replaced after the situation is stabilized.

questionText: After performing insulation resistance testing with a megohmmeter, why must the tested circuit be discharged through the megohmmeter's discharge circuit before disconnecting the test leads?

choices.text: To prevent the insulation resistance reading from being affected by residual charge in subsequent measurements
To discharge capacitive charge stored in cables and windings during the test, preventing shock hazard when removing the leads
To allow the megohmmeter to record the final reading before shutting down
To verify that the insulation has not been damaged by the test voltage

explanation: During a megohmmeter test, the high DC test voltage charges any capacitance in the circuit (distributed cable capacitance, winding capacitance, capacitor banks). When the test is complete, this stored charge can be substantial in long cable runs or transformer windings. Disconnecting the leads before discharging this stored energy through the megohmmeter's built-in discharge resistor exposes the technician to the full test voltage from the capacitive stored charge. This is a serious shock hazard. Modern megohmmeters have a discharge position that safely bleeds down the stored charge through an internal resistor before the leads are removed.

questionText: A DLRO test on a battery inter-cell connector returns 2.8mΩ. An identical connector on the same string measured 0.6mΩ on the previous PMI. What is the most likely cause and recommended action?

choices.text: Normal variation; resistance values fluctuate between tests
Loose or corroded connector; clean and re-torque the connection, then retest to confirm improvement
The DLRO test is not accurate on battery connectors; use a standard ohmmeter instead
The connector is undersized for the application; install a larger connector

explanation: A nearly 5× increase in connection resistance from 0.6mΩ to 2.8mΩ on a battery inter-cell connector between PMI cycles is a significant finding. The most common causes are corrosion at the mating surfaces (lead oxide, sulfate deposits), or the connection has loosened due to vibration or thermal cycling. The recommended action is to clean the mating surfaces with a wire brush or fine abrasive, apply a corrosion-inhibiting compound (NoOx or similar), re-torque the connector to the manufacturer's specification, and retest with the DLRO to confirm the resistance has returned to the acceptable range. High resistance connections become heat sources during discharge.

questionText: An oscilloscope connected to the DC bus in AC coupling mode shows a sinusoidal ripple at 360Hz on a 60Hz 6-pulse rectifier UPS. What is the significance of this frequency?

choices.text: 360Hz ripple is unexpected; it indicates an oscillator fault in the control circuit
360Hz is 6× the line frequency, which is the normal ripple frequency for a 6-pulse rectifier; it indicates normal rectifier operation
360Hz indicates the DC bus capacitors are resonating with the inductors at this frequency
360Hz ripple indicates one phase of the input rectifier has failed

explanation: A 6-pulse rectifier produces 6 output voltage pulses per cycle of 60Hz AC input (one positive and one negative from each of three phases = 6 pulses per 60Hz cycle). The ripple frequency equals 6 × 60Hz = 360Hz. This is the expected and normal ripple frequency for a 6-pulse rectifier. The DC bus capacitors smooth this ripple. The amplitude of the 360Hz ripple (not its frequency) is the diagnostic indicator: normal amplitude is less than 2% of DC bus voltage, and higher amplitude indicates capacitor degradation. A 12-pulse rectifier would produce ripple at 720Hz.

questionText: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between battery internal resistance and battery state of health?

choices.text: Internal resistance decreases as a battery ages, indicating improved charge transfer
Internal resistance increases as a battery ages due to corrosion, sulfation, and grid degradation; higher resistance correlates with reduced capacity
Internal resistance is constant throughout battery life and is not a useful health indicator
Internal resistance only increases during deep discharge and returns to normal after recharging

explanation: As a lead-acid battery ages, multiple degradation mechanisms increase its internal resistance: positive grid corrosion increases the resistance of current paths through the plate structure; sulfation on electrode surfaces reduces the active area available for electrochemical reactions; and electrolyte density changes affect ionic conductivity. The result is that older batteries with higher internal resistance have reduced available voltage under load (greater voltage drop under current) and deliver less effective capacity. Conductance testing exploits this relationship by measuring the inverse of resistance to trend battery state of health over time.

questionText: A UPS has two parallel battery strings. String 1 measures 48.6V at float and String 2 measures 46.2V at float. Both are 24-cell VRLA strings. What is the voltage difference per cell between the strings, and what concern does this create?

choices.text: 0.1V/cell difference; this is within acceptable tolerance for parallel strings
0.1V/cell difference; this causes circulating current between strings as the stronger string charges the weaker one
2.4V total difference; the strings are balanced because the voltage is within 5% of each other
2.4V total difference; this indicates one string is completely discharged and needs immediate replacement

explanation: String 1 at 48.6V averages 48.6/24 = 2.025V/cell. String 2 at 46.2V averages 46.2/24 = 1.925V/cell. The difference is 0.1V/cell, or 2.4V total. When two battery strings are connected in parallel at different voltages, the higher-voltage string acts as a charger for the lower-voltage string, causing circulating current to flow from String 1 to String 2. This circulating current is not controlled by the charger and can cause overcharge of String 1 and undercharge of String 2, ultimately damaging both strings. Parallel strings should be closely matched in voltage and state of charge.

questionText: A flooded lead-acid battery that has been in float service for six months shows reduced capacity in a load test. Which charging technique can partially restore capacity lost to sulfation?

choices.text: Fast charging at 2C rate for 30 minutes to dissolve sulfate crystals
Equalize charging at 2.40–2.45V/cell for several hours with adequate ventilation
Reducing float voltage to 2.00V/cell to allow natural desulfation
Equalize charging is not effective for sulfation; the battery must be replaced

explanation: Sulfation occurs when lead sulfate crystals form on battery plates during extended partial-state-of-charge operation. Equalize charging at 2.40–2.45V/cell applies a controlled overcharge that provides enough energy to dissolve some of the hardened lead sulfate crystals and restore them to the active plate material. This process is only applicable to flooded lead-acid cells where the generated gas can be vented. Early-stage sulfation can often be significantly reversed by equalize charging, though severely sulfated batteries may only achieve partial recovery. This procedure must never be used on VRLA batteries.

questionText: A technician performs an insulation test on a UPS output cable run and obtains 45MΩ. How should this reading be classified, and what action is appropriate?

choices.text: Excellent insulation; 45MΩ is above the 10MΩ threshold for acceptable insulation
Questionable insulation; 45MΩ is below the 100MΩ good-insulation threshold and warrants investigation and trending
Dangerous insulation; the cable must be replaced immediately
Acceptable insulation; cables always read lower than transformers due to longer runs

explanation: Using standard insulation resistance classifications: above 100MΩ is good, 1–100MΩ is questionable and requires investigation, and below 1MΩ is dangerous. A reading of 45MΩ falls in the questionable range. It does not require immediate shutdown (that threshold is below 1MΩ), but it does indicate that the insulation is degraded and warrants further investigation: checking for moisture ingress, physical damage, or contamination. The reading should also be compared to previous test results to evaluate the trend. If this is a declining trend, increased monitoring frequency or cable replacement planning is appropriate.

questionText: During the functional test phase of a UPS PMI, the transfer-to-battery test is initiated and the UPS transfers to battery normally. However, after 30 seconds on battery, the UPS output goes to bypass and generates a low battery alarm. The battery was just replaced six months ago. What is the most likely cause?

choices.text: The new batteries have developed early failure due to thermal runaway
The battery was not fully charged before the transfer test; a battery that has only been on float for months after installation may not be at full capacity if not given adequate initial charge
The UPS low-battery alarm setpoint is miscalibrated
The battery string has a reversed cell from improper installation

explanation: New VRLA batteries require an initial formation charge cycle and typically take 24–72 hours to reach full rated capacity on float after installation. If the batteries were never given an initial conditioning charge, or if the float voltage is slightly below specification, the batteries may appear charged by voltage measurement but actually have lower than rated capacity. Additionally, if the previous technician did not verify float voltage setpoint after installation, the batteries may have been maintained at a non-optimal charge level. The correct diagnostic step is to verify the charger float voltage setpoint and check the battery installation records for the initial charge procedure.

questionText: Using a clamp meter, a technician measures UPS input currents as: Phase A = 85A, Phase B = 84A, Phase C = 86A. The UPS nameplate shows rated input current of 90A per phase at full load. The UPS is reporting 95% load. What discrepancy needs to be investigated?

choices.text: No discrepancy; the readings are consistent with 95% load on a 90A rated UPS
The measured currents are too low for 95% load; the UPS may have a faulty current sensing circuit or is actually at a lower load than displayed
The phase imbalance exceeds 5% and must be corrected before investigating load
The UPS is drawing more current than its nameplate rating and must be derated

explanation: At 95% of rated load on a 90A/phase UPS, expected current would be approximately 0.95 × 90A = 85.5A per phase. The measured currents of 85–86A are actually consistent with 95% load, but this question tests careful analysis. The phase balance is excellent (85–86A range = less than 1.5% imbalance). If actual kVA calculations from voltage × current × power factor are compared to the UPS internal reading and they match, the reading is valid. The technician should verify by measuring voltage and calculating apparent power independently to validate the 95% load reading matches the measured currents before concluding there is a discrepancy.

questionText: A temperature-compensated UPS battery charger is set to −0.003V/°C/cell with a base temperature of 25°C. If the battery room temperature drops to 15°C, what happens to the charger output voltage per cell?

choices.text: The float voltage decreases by 0.030V/cell to compensate for lower temperature
The float voltage increases by 0.030V/cell because at lower temperatures batteries need higher voltage to accept the same charge
The float voltage is unchanged; temperature compensation only applies to temperatures above 25°C
The charger shuts down below 20°C to protect the batteries

explanation: Temperature compensation works symmetrically in both directions. The temperature coefficient is −0.003V/°C/cell, meaning for every 1°C decrease below the base temperature, the float voltage increases by 0.003V/cell. At 15°C (10°C below the 25°C base), the correction is +0.030V/cell. This counteracts the effect of cold temperature, which reduces a battery's ability to accept charge at the standard float voltage. Without this increase, a cold battery would be chronically undercharged. This is why temperature compensation uses a negative coefficient — as temperature decreases, voltage must increase (and vice versa).

questionText: During a Li-ion UPS battery inspection, the technician notices the BMS is reporting a cell temperature of 48°C while ambient is 25°C. All other cells are at 27°C. What is the correct interpretation and action?

choices.text: A 23°C rise in one cell is within normal variation; Li-ion cells can operate at 80°C
The elevated cell temperature indicates possible internal short circuit, early thermal runaway, or high internal resistance in that cell; isolate the battery cabinet immediately
The BMS temperature sensor for that cell is likely faulty; replace the sensor
Reduce the ambient temperature of the battery room to bring the cell temperature down

explanation: A single Li-ion cell running 21°C above all other cells in the same ambient environment is a serious abnormality. While normal cell temperature should be within a few degrees of adjacent cells under the same charge/discharge conditions, a 21°C delta indicates that cell is generating significantly more heat than its neighbors. This is a warning sign of internal short circuit, high internal resistance (causing more I²R heating), or early-stage thermal runaway. Li-ion thermal runaway can escalate rapidly. The correct immediate action is to isolate the battery cabinet, remove charging current, and do not leave the battery unattended until it has been inspected by qualified personnel or the temperature normalizes.

questionText: During the restore-to-service phase of a UPS PMI, the technician forgets to remove a jumper that was installed to bypass a battery protection fuse during testing. What is the consequence of this error?

choices.text: No consequence; the jumper provides a redundant path and improves reliability
The battery protection fuse is bypassed, removing overcurrent protection from the battery circuit; a battery short circuit could cause an uncontrolled fault without tripping the fuse
The jumper will cause the UPS to remain in bypass mode indefinitely
The UPS will detect the jumper and generate a fault alarm automatically

explanation: Battery protection fuses are the last line of defense against catastrophic battery short circuit current, which can exceed tens of thousands of amperes in a large battery string. Leaving a jumper across a battery fuse removes this protection entirely. If a fault occurs in the battery circuit downstream of the jumper, the full short-circuit current of the battery will flow unimpeded, potentially causing cable fires, explosive arc flash, and equipment destruction. The restore-to-service checklist must include verification that all test jumpers, shorting wires, and temporary connections have been removed before returning the system to service.

questionText: When performing a DLRO test on a busbar connection, the technician must ensure which condition is met before applying the test current?

choices.text: The busbar must be energized at normal operating voltage to simulate real-world conditions
The busbar must be de-energized, isolated under LOTO, and the DLRO current leads must make firm contact before applying test current
The DLRO can be used on energized busbars if the test current is kept below 1A
The busbar temperature must be above 20°C to ensure stable resistance readings

explanation: DLRO testing injects a known DC test current (commonly 10A or higher) through the device under test. This must be performed on de-energized, LOTO-isolated equipment for two reasons: first, for personnel safety; second, because the presence of operational currents in the busbar would interfere with the precision voltage measurement used to calculate resistance, producing inaccurate results. The test current leads must make firm, clean contact to ensure all test current flows through the measurement path, and the sensing leads must be clean and tight to detect the small millivolt signals produced by the low resistance under test.

questionText: A 20-cell VRLA battery string is on float charge at 2.26V/cell. Three cells must be temporarily removed for testing. What is the new string voltage with only 17 cells, assuming all remaining cells are still at 2.26V/cell?
explanation: With 17 cells remaining at 2.26V/cell: 17 × 2.26V = 38.42V. This is a significant reduction from the nominal 20-cell string voltage of 20 × 2.26V = 45.2V. This calculation is relevant because removing cells from a series string reduces the total string voltage proportionally. If the charger is still connected and set for the 20-cell float voltage, it will attempt to charge the 17-cell string to 45.2V, resulting in each remaining cell being overcharged to 45.2/17 = 2.66V/cell, which is well above the VRLA safe float range and could cause thermal runaway in the remaining cells.

questionText: During a battery capacity test, the test is terminated early because one cell in the string reaches 1.75V while the string is still at 80% of its rated discharge time. All other cells measure above 1.90V. What does this finding indicate?

choices.text: The test is invalid; a single-cell endpoint is not a valid termination criterion
One cell has significantly less capacity than the others; this is the weak cell that limits the entire string and the full string should be replaced
The battery string passed the test because it lasted 80% of rated time
The cell reached endpoint due to a higher discharge rate; redistribute the load evenly

explanation: IEEE 1188 specifies that a capacity test is terminated when either the average string voltage or any individual cell reaches the end-of-discharge voltage (1.75V/cell). A single cell reaching endpoint at 80% of rated time while all others are still well above 1.90V clearly identifies that cell as the capacity-limiting weak cell in the string. The fact that the test reached 80% of rated time does not mean it passed — it reached the single-cell endpoint criterion at exactly 80%, which means the string delivered only 80% of its rated capacity. Since 80% equals exactly the replacement threshold, the string is at the borderline and the identified weak cell's condition justifies full string replacement.

questionText: A clamp meter measurement on the UPS input shows 82A on Phase A, 83A on Phase B, and 82A on Phase C. The input line voltage is 480V L-L. Assuming unity power factor for this calculation, what is the approximate 3-phase input apparent power in kVA?

choices.text: Approximately 68 kVA
Approximately 118 kVA
Approximately 59 kVA
Approximately 136 kVA

explanation: For a balanced 3-phase system: S = √3 × V(L-L) × I = 1.732 × 480V × 82.3A (average current) = 1.732 × 480 × 82.3 ≈ 68,400 VA ≈ 68 kVA. The average current is (82 + 83 + 82) / 3 = 82.3A. This calculation gives the apparent power. Actual kW would require multiplying by the power factor of the load. Clamp meter current measurements combined with voltage measurements allow field verification of UPS loading that can be compared to the UPS internal load display, providing an independent check on the UPS metering accuracy.

questionText: A Ni-Cd battery bank for a critical UPS has been maintained in float charge without full discharge cycles for two years. The site reports that UPS runtime on battery has decreased significantly. What is the most likely cause and recommended corrective action?

choices.text: The batteries have sulfated; perform an equalize charge at 2.40–2.45V/cell
Memory effect from chronic float without full discharge; perform one or more complete discharge-to-endpoint then full recharge cycles to partially restore capacity
The Ni-Cd batteries have failed; replace them with VRLA batteries
The charger float voltage is too low; increase float voltage to 1.45V/cell

explanation: The memory effect is the primary capacity-reduction mechanism in Ni-Cd batteries that are repeatedly floated without full discharge. Unlike lead-acid batteries that suffer from sulfation, Ni-Cd batteries develop crystalline formation of cadmium hydroxide at the repeated shallow discharge endpoint, reducing available capacity. The remedy is performing one to three complete discharge cycles (down to the manufacturer's specified endpoint voltage) followed by full recharge. This process can recover most of the capacity lost to memory effect, though severely affected cells may only partially recover. Sulfation is specific to lead-acid chemistry, not Ni-Cd.

questionText: An oscilloscope connected to a UPS DC bus shows ripple voltage at both 360Hz and 120Hz simultaneously. The 120Hz component is new compared to the previous PMI measurement. What is the most likely source of the 120Hz ripple component?

choices.text: The 120Hz component is a harmonic of the 360Hz fundamental; it is always present
One phase of the 3-phase, 6-pulse input rectifier has failed or has a significantly degraded diode, causing uneven 3-phase rectification
The DC bus capacitors have a resonance frequency of 120Hz
The 120Hz component is from the battery charger switching at twice the line frequency

explanation: A properly functioning 6-pulse 3-phase rectifier produces 360Hz ripple (6× line frequency). If one phase of the input is missing or one rectifier diode has failed/degraded, the rectifier effectively becomes unbalanced. A failed positive or negative diode in one phase causes the corresponding pulse to be missing or reduced, disrupting the 360Hz pattern and introducing lower-frequency components including 120Hz (2× line frequency), which is characteristic of single-phase or unbalanced 3-phase rectification. The appearance of 120Hz ripple that was not present in previous measurements strongly suggests a rectifier diode failure or input phase loss condition requiring immediate investigation.

questionText: Multiple technicians are working on the same UPS simultaneously. Under OSHA LOTO regulations, how must the lockout procedure be handled?

choices.text: One technician applies the group lock and all others trust that the equipment is safe
Each authorized employee working on the equipment must apply their own personal lock to each lockout point using a hasp
The crew leader applies locks for the entire crew; individual locks are not required
A tagout device is sufficient when multiple technicians are present, as it notifies others not to operate the equipment

explanation: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 group lockout requirements specify that when more than one employee is working on the same equipment, each authorized employee must have their own personal lock applied to a hasp or lockout box at each energy isolation point. This ensures that the equipment cannot be re-energized until every individual worker has removed their personal lock, indicating they are clear of the hazard zone. Using a single lock (even with a hasp) that one person controls, or relying on tagout alone for a multi-person job, violates the requirement that each worker controls their own personal safety through their own lock.

questionText: A battery conductance test instrument shows that a 12-cell VRLA string has an average conductance of 240S/cell. The manufacturer baseline for new cells is 300S. Individual cell readings range from 195S to 265S. Which cells are at or below the typical replacement threshold, and what is the overall string recommendation?

choices.text: Only cells below 200S should be replaced individually; others are within range
Cells at 195S (65% of baseline) are clearly below threshold; the string average at 80% of baseline is at the replacement threshold; recommend full string replacement
The string is healthy; 240S average is above the 200S minimum acceptable level
No action needed; the string should be retested after an equalize charge

explanation: The replacement threshold is typically 75–80% of the manufacturer's baseline conductance. At 80% of 300S = 240S, this string average is exactly at the lower boundary of the replacement threshold range. The lowest individual cell at 195S = 65% of baseline is clearly below the threshold and will limit string performance. Since the string average is at the replacement threshold, has at least one cell well below threshold, and the range (195S–265S) shows significant variation indicating non-uniform aging, full string replacement is the appropriate recommendation. Replacing individual cells would leave the new cell paired with cells already at end-of-life.

questionText: During the mechanical checks phase of a UPS PMI, a technician discovers that two cooling fans are not rotating. The UPS is currently on-line supplying critical load. What is the correct procedure?

choices.text: Leave the fans as-is and document the finding for the next scheduled PMI
Immediately transfer the UPS to bypass to de-energize it for fan replacement

Assess the thermal risk: check UPS operating temperature and derating requirements, notify the customer of the fault, and work with the customer to schedule the fastest possible maintenance window for fan replacement while monitoring temperature
Replace the fans immediately while the UPS is energized without any additional safety steps

explanation: Failed cooling fans on an online UPS create a thermal risk that must be managed carefully. The immediate actions are: check the UPS temperature display for signs of overheating, determine if the UPS manufacturer's specs require derating (reducing load) with fewer fans operational, notify the customer and document the fault, and assess whether the current thermal conditions are safe for continued operation while a maintenance window is arranged. Simply leaving failed fans undocumented is unacceptable. Immediately transferring to bypass without customer approval disrupts critical load. The balance is managed risk notification and expedited repair scheduling while monitoring thermal status.

questionText: A UPS charger float voltage is set to 54.0V for a 24-cell VRLA string. A field tech calculates the per-cell voltage and determines it is outside the acceptable VRLA float range. What is the per-cell voltage and what adjustment is needed?

choices.text: 2.25V/cell; this is within the acceptable VRLA float range of 2.25–2.27V/cell, no adjustment needed
2.25V/cell; this is at the low end of the acceptable range; increase to 54.24–54.48V for better float maintenance
2.20V/cell; below the 2.25V minimum; increase float voltage to at least 54.0V
2.30V/cell; above the 2.27V maximum; reduce float voltage to 54.0–54.48V range

explanation: Per-cell voltage = 54.0V / 24 cells = 2.25V/cell. The acceptable VRLA float voltage range is 2.25–2.27V/cell. A reading of exactly 2.25V/cell is at the lower boundary of the acceptable range, so the charger setting is technically within specification, though some engineers prefer the middle of the range around 2.26V/cell. No adjustment is strictly required, but the technician might choose to increase slightly to 54.24V (2.26V/cell) to ensure reliable maintenance at the lower end of normal temperature variation. This question tests the ability to perform the per-cell calculation and compare the result to the VRLA specification.

questionText: A technician performs back-to-back insulation resistance tests on the same UPS transformer winding, one hour apart, under identical conditions. The first test reads 320MΩ and the second reads 310MΩ. What is the correct interpretation?

choices.text: The 10MΩ decrease indicates significant insulation degradation and the transformer should be taken out of service
The readings are essentially the same; a 3% variation is within normal test repeatability and both readings indicate good insulation above 100MΩ
The decrease confirms the first test damaged the insulation; limit test duration to under 30 seconds
The second reading is invalid because insulation resistance tests cannot be repeated within 24 hours

explanation: Both readings of 320MΩ and 310MΩ are well above the 100MΩ threshold for good insulation. A 10MΩ variation between tests represents only about 3% of the reading, which is well within the normal repeatability of megohmmeter measurements. Factors including slight temperature differences, humidity changes, and instrument measurement uncertainty can produce variations of this magnitude. Insulation resistance readings are used for trending over months or years, not for making pass/fail decisions based on small variations between same-day tests. A concerning trend would be measurements declining from hundreds of MΩ to tens of MΩ over successive annual tests.
